author: Tyler Swenson

children.text: The documents required for selling inherited property can feel overwhelming when you're already dealing with grief.
. Affidavit of heirship. Tax clearance certificates.
And if you get it wrong, the sale falls apart.
I'm Tyler Swenson, founder of ClearEdge Home Buyers.
Since 2016, I've helped dozens of families across Eastern Pennsylvania sell inherited properties.
I've seen deals delayed for months because of missing paperwork.
I've also seen families close in two weeks because they had everything lined up.
The difference? Knowing exactly what documents you need upfront.
Let me walk you through the complete checklist.
The Two Paths: Probate vs. Non-Probate
Before we dive into documents, you need to know which situation you're in.
Path 1: The property went through probate (or is currently in probate)
— This is most common. If the house was solely in the deceased's name, probate is required.
— This happens if the property was in a living trust, had a Transfer on Death deed, or was jointly owned with right of survivorship.
Your document requirements depend on which path you're on.
Documents Required for Selling Inherited Property (Probate Path)
If you're going through probate — or already went through it — here's what you'll need to sell.
Official document proving the property owner passed away.
Pennsylvania Department of Health, local register of wills, or the funeral home.
Get at least 5-6 certified copies. Banks, title companies, and buyers all want originals.
About $20 per certified copy in Pennsylvania.
Order more than you think you need. Running out mid-transaction is a headache.
Court document proving you have legal authority to act on behalf of the estate.
= There was a will, and you're the named executor.
= No will existed, and the court appointed you as administrator.
The Register of Wills in the county where the deceased lived.
Without this, you cannot legally sell the property. Period.
The deceased's legal document stating how assets should be distributed.
The original should be filed with the Register of Wills during probate.
The will may specify how real estate should be handled. Some wills give executors full authority to sell. Others require court approval.
Government-issued photo ID (driver's license or passport), Social Security number
Title companies verify your identity before transferring property.
Legal document showing current ownership of the property.
County Recorder of Deeds office, or from the deceased's files.
This confirms the property was owned by the deceased and shows how it was titled.
A search of public records to verify ownership and uncover any liens, mortgages, or claims against the property.
Usually the title company or closing attorney handles this.
Clears the path for clean title transfer to the buyer.
Certificate from Pennsylvania Department of Revenue showing
es have been paid or are accounted for.
File Form REV-1500 with PA Department of Revenue.
is due within 9 months of death. Title companies want proof this is handled before closing.
0% for surviving spouses, 4.5% for direct descendants (children, grandchildren), 12% for siblings, 15% for others
Proof that property taxes are current.
Outstanding property taxes create liens. These must be cleared at closing.
Statement from the lender showing exact amount needed to pay off any remaining mortgage.
The mortgage gets paid from sale proceeds at closing.
Formal court approval to sell the real estate.
If the will doesn't give the executor independent authority to sell, or if heirs dispute the sale.
File a petition with the Orphans' Court in the county where probate is happening.
Documents Required for Selling Inherited Property (Non-Probate Path)
If the property bypassed probate, your document list is shorter.
If Property Was in a Living Trust:
Death certificate (certified copy), The trust document (shows you as successor trustee), Trustee certification (affidavit confirming your authority), Property deed (showing trust ownership), Your ID
If Property Had a Transfer on Death (TOD) Deed:
Death certificate (certified copy), The original TOD deed (recorded with the county), Affidavit of survivorship (confirms you're the named beneficiary), Your ID
If Property Was Jointly Owned (Right of Survivorship):
Death certificate (certified copy), The original deed (showing joint tenancy with right of survivorship), Affidavit of survivorship (to remove deceased from title), Your ID
In all these cases, no court involvement is needed. You update the title, then sell.
Beyond proving your authority to sell, buyers (or their lenders) typically request:
— Shows exact boundaries and any encroachments
— PA requires sellers to disclose known defects
— If applicable, includes rules and financials
Cash buyers like us don't require most of this. We buy as-is. No inspection contingencies. No lengthy disclosure negotiations. Just the documents proving you can legally sell.
How to Get Documents When You're Out of State
Selling an inherited property from across the country? This is more common than you'd think.
Here's how to handle the paperwork remotely:
— Even if you're in Texas or California, you need a PA attorney for probate. They can file documents, attend court hearings, and handle local requirements.
2. Use a Title Company That Handles Remote Closings
— Many title companies offer mobile notary services (they come to you), remote online notarization (RON), and mail-away closings.
— You can give a trusted person in Pennsylvania power of attorney to sign documents on your behalf. This is common when multiple heirs are spread across the country.
4. Work With a Cash Buyer (Easiest Option)
— We regularly buy inherited properties from out-of-state sellers. We coordinate with local attorneys and title companies. You sign remotely. We handle everything else.
Common Document Mistakes That Delay Sales
I've seen these kill deals or add months to the timeline:
Mistake #1: Not getting enough death certificates
— Order 5-6 minimum. Every institution wants their own certified copy.
— Start the probate process immediately. This is the bottleneck.
Mistake #3: Not checking the title early
— Hidden liens, old mortgages, or judgment creditors can surprise you at closing. Run a title search upfront.
is due within 9 months. Miss it and you pay penalties plus interest.
— Get written agreement from all heirs before listing. One holdout can derail everything.
— If you can't find the original will, probate gets complicated. Search safe deposit boxes, attorney offices, and home safes.
Timeline: How Long Does It Take to Get These Documents?
Here's a realistic timeline for Pennsylvania:
Total time before you can sell (probate path):
Total time before you can sell (non-probate path):
This is why proper estate planning matters. Trusts and TOD deeds save your heirs months of waiting.
The estate goes through "intestate" probate. Property passes according to PA law (spouse first, then children, then other relatives).
The county Recorder of Deeds has copies of all recorded deeds. You can request a certified copy.
Order new copies from PA Department of Health or the local Register of Wills.
If probate was completed years ago, contact the Register of Wills in the county where it was filed. They keep records.
Check the deceased's mail, email, and bank statements. The servicer's name will appear somewhere.
Selling Inherited Property to a Cash Buyer: What Documents We Need
At ClearEdge Home Buyers, we've simplified this process.
When you sell to us, here's what we need:
, Your photo ID, Signed purchase agreement
Title search, Title insurance, Closing coordination, All paperwork
That's it. No bank appraisals. No buyer financing documents. No inspection reports.
We close as soon as probate allows — often within 2 weeks of receiving
The documents required for selling inherited property depend on your situation.
, the will, deed, title search, tax clearances, and possibly court approval.
Death certificate, trust document or TOD deed, affidavit of survivorship, and your ID.
Either way, start gathering documents immediately.
The sooner you have everything, the faster you can sell and move on.
If you're dealing with an inherited property in Eastern Pennsylvania and want to skip the hassle of repairs, showings, and buyer financing...
Get a free, no-obligation cash offer at ClearEdge Home Buyers or learn more about our simple 3-step process.
We've helped dozens of families navigate this exact situation.

Can You Sell Without Probate in PA?

excerpt: The documents required for selling inherited property can feel overwhelming. Here's the complete checklist for Pennsylvania — whether you're going through probate or not.

faqs.answer: Yes, once you have Letters Testamentary and authority to sell. You don't have to wait for probate to fully close.
faqs.question: Can I sell inherited property before probate is complete?

faqs.answer: All heirs typically need to sign the deed or consent to the sale. One alternative: the executor can sell on behalf of the estate if the will grants that authority.
faqs.question: What if multiple heirs inherited the property?

faqs.answer: For probate, yes — get a probate attorney. For the actual sale to a cash buyer, a lawyer isn't required but can review documents if you want.
faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer to sell inherited property?

faqs.answer: Liens get paid from sale proceeds at closing. If liens exceed the property value, you'll need to negotiate with creditors or consider a short sale.
faqs.question: What if there are liens on the property?

faqs.answer: Letters Testamentary (if there's a will) or Letters of Administration (if no will) plus the death certificate prove your authority.
faqs.question: How do I prove I'm the rightful heir?

faqs.answer: A sworn document identifying the deceased's heirs. Used when there's no will to establish who inherits.
faqs.question: What's an affidavit of heirship?

faqs.answer: You can sell before paying, but the title company will want assurance taxes will be paid from proceeds — or a tax clearance certificate.
faqs.question: Do I need to pay inheritance tax before selling?

faqs.answer: Contact the attorney who created the trust. They should have copies. Also check safe deposit boxes and the deceased's important papers.
faqs.question: What if the property is in a trust but I can't find the trust document?

metaDescription: Complete checklist of documents needed to sell inherited property in Pennsylvania. Death certificates, Letters Testamentary, tax clearances, and more.
title: Documents Required for Selling Inherited Property in Pennsylvania
